text: The surgeon inserts a 10mm umbilical port and a 10mm left iliac fossa port for laparoscopy. The laparoscopic view reveals the right and transverse colon displaced to the left side due to the volvulus, with the small bowel on the right. Two additional ports are placed: a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa and another in the epigastrium. Automatic graspers are used to gently untwist the colon in an anti-clockwise direction, repositioning the cecum to the right of the falciform ligament. The left transverse colon is elevated to create space for the small bowel, which is gradually repositioned to the left side by carefully pulling it up from the right.

The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy by making the ileocolic pedicle taut and conducting parmedial dissection in front of the duodenum. The ileocolic artery is clipped with a hammer lock and divided using an end seal. Blunt dissection is used for further medial mobilization of the colon. A tonsil swab is placed in front of the duodenum as a marker, aiding in the retrieval of the transverse colon and hepatic flexure from the superior side.

The surgeon mobilizes the appendix, cecum, ascending colon, and hepatic flexure from the lateral side, fully freeing the right colon. Despite excess redundancy in the bowel, the dissection proceeds smoothly. The colon is then extracted through the umbilical incision, and an extracorporeal anastomosis is performed.
